moment_text: I found a lump in July, 2022, just doing a regular self-breast exam at home. I've always done these routine exams and I found this lump, and honestly, I, I think if anyone's experience is the first thing you go to is like, oh, like for me, I was just like, oh, it's just a cyst and this is a common thing. And um, I was in the middle of a lot of work and a lot of travel and I didn't have a gynecologist and so. Admittedly, I put it off for about a month. And um, then when I decided to really take it serious, I went to a breast clinic in Los Angeles and you know, one thing led to another and it eventually was a lot more serious than I thought.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) as it contains multiple barrier signals (access_barrier, time_constraint) and agency signals (avoidance, seeking_care), indicating a pattern of behavior and system interaction.

moment_text: I was a personal trainer. I was working. Heavily in the fitness and the wellness space. I was planning fitness events all over the country. I was filming on camera with Amazon, with pop sugar, with all of these, I was working with all of the top like fitness and wellness brands, and I was considerably like one of the healthiest people I knew. I took really good care of my body, though. I had been experiencing a little bit of, um, health issues that I couldn't. Resolve. Um, this was like years after I was getting off of birth control and I was experiencing weight gain. I was working with all these naturopath doctors and then I finally figured it out. I was diagnosed with Hashimoto's that had been there for a few years, and so. At the time when I was diagnosed, my, my health had finally settled after all of this time, and I was like feeling really good in my body. I was living my life, I was traveling, I was having fun in Los Angeles. I was saying yes to all of these things and, and I also felt like my career after so long was finally at like its peak. I was like, I've been working so hard and I finally felt like in life I had. I really got into a really good place, like physically, financially, mentally, spiritually, all of these things.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) by detailing a prior delayed_diagnosis (barrier signal) for a different condition and the seeking_care (agency signal) that led to it, establishing a history of health navigation.
notes_for_reviewer: The delayed diagnosis mentioned here is for Hashimoto's, not the breast cancer, but it provides important context about her prior experiences with the healthcare system.

moment_text: Thinking during all of this time, you know, there is just a big fear of the unknown. There's been a fair share of cancer in my family, never breast cancer. I also lost my best guy friend when we were 25 and he was diagnosed with colon cancer and he passed pretty quickly from that. So that was very traumatic and obviously you immediately get those thoughts in your mind of like, what does this mean for my own life? What does this mean for. Me as a person, my life in general, my future, my body, my health, my career, all of these things. And it's really scary. It's a moment of just like, there's so much unknown and so much uncertainty, um, that that fear alone can really just like stop you in your tracks.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) due to its strong emotional signals (fear, anxiety, grief) linked to a system interaction (family history) and a clear barrier signal (lack of information about the future).

moment_text: Of course, being diagnosed with breast cancer at 32, I had never heard of anyone diagnosed with breast cancer at at my age. Um. Growing up, I had friends moms who had had it, or friends, grandparents, things like that. And I was like, I didn't even know that this was possible. I'm like, what do you mean I have breast cancer? Like this isn't even my family. So it was very confusing for me and also my family because we thought it was, you know, primarily genetic.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) by explicitly stating a significant education_gap (barrier signal) and the resulting confusion (emotional signal) within a family context.

moment_text: You know, when you are first diagnosed, you were basically. Thrown into the deep end, and I don't even wanna say deep end because it's not a pool, it is like the ocean. It is like someone plucks you out of your life, takes you to the middle of the ocean and it was like figure it out and drops you there and you're just like, what do I do? You know there there's nurse navigators and there's people who are like, okay, you need to do this and you need to do this. And it's kind of like you're following these little breadcrumbs to figure out how to get to the next step, into the next step.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) with clear barrier signals (system_navigation_difficulty, fragmented_care) and emotional signals (overwhelm) describing the interaction with the healthcare system.

moment_text: I had figured everything else out up until that point in my life, and so I knew that I was going to figure this out, but it didn't mean it mean it or make it any easier like it was. I was crying every single day. I was on the phone up to three times a day with insurance, with my doctors, with trying to find the right care team. It is a very stressful time, and so the logistics of trying to figure out. The next step kind of forces the emotional trauma of being diagnosed with cancer. Puts that on the back burner a little bit. So it's like you're trying to navigate the real world stuff while also processing this huge thing and what this means in your life. And you don't even know how to explain to people like, yeah, I need help, but like I'm really the only person that can. Go through this. Um, and no one explains that to you and no one teaches you like how to navigate those first few moments of, of diagnosis?

moment_text: My when, when we found out that my diagnosis had progressed to stage two, they were like, if you wanna freeze your eggs, you have one month from today. To find your doctor, go through the egg freezing process, and then we're starting chemo November 29th, and I was like, oh my God. And everything has to line up for that too. So it ended up happening perfectly. But the day before my chemo on November 28th, I had my egg retrieval, which if anyone has done egg retrieval, this is like a whole process. Right, like your body is going through it. Also, you're injecting yourself with hormones for two to three weeks, like you are not right, and then to go and then have a eight hour chemo session treatment while cold capping. My body was just like in disarray. And I was experiencing a lot of side effects after that. Like I had to go get x-rays on my belly. I thought I had an infection. I like my belly was distended, I couldn't eat. And, and then just like experiencing the physical side effects of going through ke chemo on that, on top of that was really, really hard.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) as it details a significant barrier encounter, including a time_constraint and fragmented_care, while also showing agency (making_decisions) within the healthcare system.

moment_text: So I'll say like off of the, off the bat, like the physical stuff was really hard, but the emotional deep down is the hardest thing. To process and learn how to navigate, especially if you haven't experienced major emotional hardships or turmoil in your life, because this is gonna be the one thing that is gonna uproot everything in your life and really ask you to sit with something that's really, really challenging.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate (A2) by identifying a significant barrier (lack_of_support in navigating emotions) and the agency required to face it (enduring), highlighting a critical aspect of the patient experience.

moment_text: No, I, I don't think that it was explained to me. I am so grateful and so blessed with a huge network of people in my life being in this space and just having a big range of people that work in so many different areas. And I was with a couple girlfriends prior to my. Um, breast cancer progressing to stage two. And they were the ones who told me, listen, if you have to go through chemotherapy, make sure you tell your doctor that you want to freeze your eggs because once you start chemotherapy, it's gonna be too late 'cause you can become infertile. My oncologist who had um, uh, diagnosed me, never said that to me, never said to me, you know, Hey, this is an option.

moment_text: And where I was diagnosed. Once it had progressed to stage two, I immediately was like, I really wanna have kids. I really wanna freeze my eggs. And the first thing he said to me was, you have BRCA two, which I'm BRCA two. He's like, you have BRCA two. Are you sure you wanna pass that down to your kids? And I was like, what? I was like, okay, I immediately need to change providers. I need to go to a new place. But he didn't even present that as an option, so I was. So grateful that I was able to come to him and, and ask that question.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible due to its clear demonstration of both a significant barrier (provider dismissal and judgment) and strong patient agency (changing providers, self-advocacy) within a healthcare context.
notes_for_reviewer: The provider's response about passing down the BRCA2 gene is a stark example of dismissal and judgment.

moment_text: I don't think so, to be honest. I feel like I was very lucky with my treatment with my care team. I'm a very intuitive person, so I usually go to people that I feel aligned with or that, um, there's a sense of comfort there and. The care team that I moved to immediately, it felt like they really had my best interests at heart. And so, um, I kind of just trusted them. And I also knew that there was a standard of care according to my diagnosis. And then I got a second opinion at another incredible, um, facility, and they gave me the exact same care plan. And so I was like, you know what? I'm gonna go where I feel emotionally supported because I know that the treatment plan is gonna be the same. So I feel very. Blessed that I didn't really have to push so, so hard.

moment_text: No, I really have to think about this because I'm just like, what was easy for me? I think the easiest part, and this is gonna sound weird, was. Myself navigating how I showed up in this world and in this experience, and in my body and in my mind, like I was like, I have no control over everything that's happening around me and to me, but the only control that I can have is within myself. And I think that that was easy for me to navigate because I've always been someone. That is choosing to do the work and choosing to become better out of any experience in my life and really making a choice to shift my perspective on what was happening was the best thing for me in that whole experience because it's very easy. For people to choose the low hanging fruit of like, this is really hard, this is really miserable, this is really painful, this is really exhausting. The list goes on and on and on, like, right? Like you can always find those things. But in that moment, I made a choice to say like, what is this? Teaching me what are the lessons that are coming out of this and where am I being asked to step up and grow from this?

moment_text: There were a couple people that reached out to me that were honestly lifesavers at the beginning when I shared it online. There were people that came out of the woodworks and really helped me navigate the journey and what's to come across the board for so many different topics. Cold capping, chemo, you know, and in next stages. And that was really helpful for me 'cause I felt like I was just navigating this in the dark. But you know, when you start to share your story, so many people step up. To the plate to help you. And that was also why I chose to share my entire journey. From start to finish, I started filming myself 'cause I was already starting to make contact wellness content around Hashimoto's and fitness and mindset and all of these things, but. The night before my diagnosis, I just had this feeling that I was gonna be, that it was breast cancer. And I started, I started filming it from there and I shared the whole journey because I was like, I don't know what this means to go through chemo and go through breast cancer at 32. And I know I'm not the gonna be the last person. So I'm like, if someone. Is following me. If I can help one person understand this journey that doesn't have anyone in their life, then this is my opportunity to do this. Um, and so yeah, there was a few people that really helped me as I was navigating this with a flashlight and then I was able to shine a light on the whole experience to help other people.

moment_text: Yes, it really did. Um, and I don't just say that as like putting a positive spin to it. I believe that. I was supposed to have breast cancer. I truly believe that. I feel like it was something that my soul chose to experience on this earth and that there is huge purpose behind it. Prior to breast cancer and growing up, I always hated my breast. I hated my breasts. I was at war with my body. I was never thin enough. I was never fit enough. I was never this, I was never that. And the breasts were also something that I just did not like. I had larger breasts my whole life and, um, all my friends had smaller boobs and my whole family had smaller breasts. And so it was something that I was always like, I wanna get a reduction and I want these to be gone. And also emotionally, um, I had experienced, you know, I had experienced some. Trauma as a kid, and I believe that the trauma lived in my breast, so it was something that I was forever pushing away. And so when breast cancer came. And I now, it was, I was having to get treatment to the very thing that I was energetically, emotionally, physically pushing out of my life. And now I was having to go through a double mastectomy where they were being removed from my body without my say and without my control. It was then now another opportunity for me to be like, okay, are you going to love yourself now? Now we removed this from your body without you actually really wanting them to be gone, even though you've been pushing them away your whole life. But now, are you gonna love your body? Now we're forcing this illness to be centered around this very thing that you've been pushing away. Okay, are you gonna come back and are you gonna love yourself now? And that was a huge. A huge lesson for me, aside from everything else physically, but I think that that was one of the biggest things for me.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible due to its deep reflection on body image and self-worth, containing powerful emotional signals (shame, empowerment) and agency signals (self-blame, making decisions), representing a significant emotional pattern.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker connects her breast cancer to past trauma and a lifelong negative relationship with her body, framing the illness as a catalyst for self-love.

moment_text: I am so grateful to be here in this life. I feel like I have. UPS leveled every aspect of my life, but primarily on a soul level of like who I am today. I look, I was just looking back at photos of myself pre-cancer 'cause I was like doing a little social post and I was just like, that woman was so amazing. But I am like light years ahead of where she was. And this was just a couple years ago and I'm so grateful for that. You know, you really have to go through some stuff in your life and really do a lot of work on yourself to be able to go through something like that and come out better on the other side. And so I am so grateful. But in the second part, I feel like I, I am still moving through this life and there's still a lot left that I want to achieve and I want to have. For things to happen in my life as well too. You know, I'm, I'm still single and I still really want that relationship and that family and my career. I'm still building and I'm growing and I'm in a much better place than I was, but I'm still not there yet. Um. And that's the one thing about cancer that's really hard is I feel like when you're going through treatment, you feel like your life comes through a screeching halt and everyone's life is kind of moving by you while you're at the standstill.

moment_text: Just being like, I can't wait to be back out there and play with my friends again and, and go back to life. Like I feel like I've been running to try to catch up to everyone around me in this version of me that I feel like I should be at.

moment_text: And so that is a lesson that I'm currently learning is like, there is no specific timeline. I'm exactly where I need to be, and it's, it's hard like trusting that. So yes, I'm happy, but I'm, I'm still trying to navigate what this is all supposed to look like and be, you know?
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains agency signals (enduring) and emotional signals (self_doubt, confusion).

moment_text: I'm around people all the time. I'm, I'm a coach, I'm a personal trainer, I'm a group fitness instructor, and then I'm also the founder of Cancer Bodies and I'm on social media. So people see a perception of me. That is not always exactly how I feel. Um, you know, I have to put on a brave face and I have to smile and I encourage a lot of people, but there's a lot of stuff that I'm navigating deep down.

moment_text: Um, there's a lot of the treatment that I'm still processing, a lot of the emotions that I'm still working through, because doctors don't tell you that when you get to survivorship, that's actually when you. eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains multiple barrier signals (lack_of_information, education_gap) related to a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Straight answer. No. I really don't think that there is enough support on the other side. But what you don't realize is like that light at the end of the tunnel is the start to a new tunnel that you're navigating. Um, and. That isn't their job to help you navigate that part because they're just trying to help you survive. So it's really important that there are things that are set up and there are resources and organizations and. People that are there to catch you once you get to that, that quote unquote finish line.

moment_text: But navigating it at a young age is, is really hard. It was all about, you know, proving myself through work, looking for love, saying yes to everything, not wasting my twenties, and now quality of life is. Resting, loving myself first saying no, you know, really doing the things that like light my soul on fire and tending to me.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains multiple agency signals (setting_boundaries, making_decisions) and emotional signals (empowerment, confidence).

moment_text: You know what? I think supporting the community of people that like need other support. I think it's easy for me to share my experience to help other people. And so when people come to me and they're like. What did you do for this? What do I do for this? Like, where can you support me? That is easy for me to manage because I'm just sharing my experience knowing that that will help someone else. I think there's a lot of really challenging aspects of all of the things that you need to navigate through in survivorship. Um, but I think the one thing that makes this whole journey worthwhile is having the ability to. Support someone else on theirs.

moment_text: I think for me personally, the pressure that I put on myself to catch up to these expectations that I've had on my life. So. I am a very high achieving person. I'm a perfectionist. I like to bury myself in work, but finding that balance of. Going back to what I said before of like having that quality of life, of taking time off, of resting, of doing the things that really light my soul on fire and navigating that stress that I put on myself to achieve and to do more has been really hard. I'm like, I can't repeat these old patterns.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains multiple emotional signals (anxiety, overwhelm) and agency signals (self_blame, enduring).

moment_text: I, I really think the. The people that helped me the most. I didn't necessarily have like one mentor, but I really think as I was sharing and building a community online. These people thought I was helping them, but it was really healing for me. Like I really leaned into sharing my journey because when I would post a video and then I would get all of the comments of these people that were also experiencing the exact same thing, it really made me feel like I wasn't alone.

moment_text: Going down to genetic testing of it all. I had never even heard of genetic testing before my diagnosis. And I think if I would've been informed that I was BRCA two positive, there were would've been other preventative measures put in place for me to know my risks and understand, um, what it all meant for me. Because by the time I figured it out, I was already diagnosed, I think.

moment_text: Um. Treatment while you're going through treatment, the resources that are available to younger people. Um, you know, for the longest time breast cancer has been a cancer for older generations. But now as research is proving, a lot of younger women are being diagnosed, and this is a very. Huge, what I like to call epidemic of young women being diagnosed. And so I think that there needs to be a lot more resources and availability for healing spaces, for healing for young people. And not just the physical, but like while the doctors are, are tuning into the physical. What about the mental health of it all? What about the emotional health of it all? What about like.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains multiple barrier signals (lack_of_support, fragmented_care) and agency signals (speaking_up) related to a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: At all areas of our life as a young person, how do we navigate that? The dating of it all, like the real world topics that are never addressed with our care team because they're not, you know, they're not versed in all of that. They don't have the time to, to assist you in that. Um, and that also goes into like survivorship as well too. Like what about like, total wellness? For me, I've always been about. All four pillars of wellness, the physical, mental, emotional, spiritual health. When we are not supporting at least one of those, like how can we fully function, I view it as like four wheels on a tire. If one of those wheels is down, you can't drive your car.

moment_text: And it's the same thing when going through cancer or just life in general. And I wish that there were more well-rounded tools to help navigate that and heal through all of those components. Absolutely. I, I think, um, and this is just my commentary, I, I think the care system hits it out of the park with nurse navigators, and you mentioned it earlier, but like, it's overwhelming. It having a way to understand like, this appointment comes before this one and whatnot. Like, it makes a big difference, but the biggest gap is. What comes after, because that's where we have the longest runway.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with barrier signals (fragmented_care, lack_of_support) and a clear system interaction with a healthcare_provider.

moment_text: I truly believe getting in your body will connect you to your body, so. There's a big misconception about fatigue, and it's like the more fatigue you have, the more rest you need to have. And it's actually quite the opposite. But I do believe there's a fine line, right? I believe there's a fine line of understanding where you're at, allowing yourself rest, but also moving your body. Exercise gives you energy and people don't realize that they think that it wipes you out. And some movement does does, but we're not focusing on that type of movement. I think. Starting really small for me with treatment, especially on chemo weeks. I got chemo once every three weeks. The first week my goal was one or two walks around the block around the neighborhood. You get fresh air, you get to see people, you get to move your body a little bit and, and things are moving through you. And then subsequent weeks after that was really focusing on strength training, building my strength. Um, I was still doing high intensity workouts whenever I was feeling good. But again, I was. Tuning into what my body was asking of me, um, and what I was needing. And I think the more pleasurable the movement is for you starting with that, the more you'll be encouraged to do that. And so the more you connect with your physical shell, the more you be able to build that relationship. So that's step one is like doing baby steps with that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with both agency signals (making_decisions) and barrier signals (lack_of_information).
notes_for_reviewer: The barrier 'lack_of_information' is tied to the 'big misconception about fatigue' that the speaker is correcting.

moment_text: And then step two. Which I actually hated, but it really helped with doing mirror work with my new breasts. So after I got expanders and then eventually I got the implants, your body. It doesn't feel like your own. You are looking at yourself in your mirror and you're just like, I don't even recognize myself. And my, um, plastic surgeons were like, okay, you can touch yourself. But I was like, I don't wanna just like touch myself. So I had one breast because of radiation that was really high, really tight and really dark because of the radiation recall. So for me, I started putting skincare. On my breasts and massaging the breasts to really like loosen it up. And also just my goal was like, okay, it's really dark. I wanna put vitamin C on there. I wanna put some like lightning stuff on there. Once I was approved to do all of that. And then as I was doing that, I didn't realize that subconsciously and emotionally I was. Connecting to my new breasts, and I was also unwiring a lot of these negative relationships that I spoke about earlier that I had with my breasts. And so I was doing all of these amazing things and then physically, my breasts were changing, my breasts were dropping. It was lightning. It looks completely different now than what it did before, but I think the most beneficial thing that I received of it was like that connection that I, I was creating to not only like my soul, but my physical body as well.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with multiple emotional signals (connection, confusion) and agency signals (making_decisions) within a healthcare_provider context.

moment_text: Yeah. Wow. Thank you. That's full circle. And like the mind body connection is so powerful and I, I think it's underestimated how it can get severed, uh, in this process. But like really fighting or being intentional, um, to get that back is important. And it has me thinking of things that I wanna get back into as well.
eligibility_rationale: Fails A2 gate. The moment contains an emotional signal but lacks any barrier or agency signals, and the system interaction is 'none'.

moment_text: I believe advocacy is important because I fully believe that education is power. I think the more education that you have, the more awareness that you have about. Anything and everything, um, gives you more strength. So something that I speak about a lot in cancer bodies is we love to talk about like taboo quote unquote taboo topics that a lot of people are uncomfortable with. Me as a person. I love that I'm, I've a lot of Scorpio in my chart, so I'm like, I like to lift the covers and, and get into the nitty gritty of things, but. I don't do that because I, it's of interest of me. I do it because we are all experiencing these things, but no one is talking about it. Um, say sexual intimacy, for example, vaginal dryness, for example, as someone who was 32 and was going through treatment was single. But when I was emerging from treatment, I wanted to have a dating life. I wanted to have like a sexual relationship with someone, but my doctors. Didn't talk to me about these topics and I didn't know where to turn. I would go to the OB, GYN and they wouldn't really give me answers. And I was like, you know what? I'm gonna find these answers and then I'm gonna start talking about it, and I'm gonna create a safe space where people feel like, okay, I can share what I'm going through. Because knowledge again, is power. When when you share, you're giving space and the, and the safety for someone else to feel comfortable sharing.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with multiple strong barrier signals (lack_of_information, silence) and agency signals (self_advocacy, speaking_up) within a healthcare_provider context.

moment_text: Yes, because I think our generation is very different than our parents' generation and their parents' generation, and I think the generation also that's coming up is gonna be very different than our generation. But I think we are not ones to shy away from the truth. And being honest, and that is needed when it comes to advocacy. We can't just put everything into a nice, beautiful bow and present it and be like, okay, this is what's needed. It's like, no, let's open the package. Let's rip this open, let's throw the bow away and show you the realness of what's actually going on so that we can really make change. Um, and I think that that is the beauty of our generation and the younger generations is that like we like. Real shit, and we are gonna make stuff happen. We're not gonna take no as an answer and we're gonna just like, keep going. And that's what I love.

moment_text: My lesson from this and from any experience in life is look for the lesson in this. Like this is not something that just comes into your life like a thief in the night and is here to rob you of all of your joy and your experiences and who you are as a person. Cancer is our teacher. It is here to guide you, and you are here to be a student, to understand what the purpose and what the meaning of this is. There's a reason it came in and you. Are not supposed to repeat these same lessons that were happening before. So really look for that and really try to flip your perspective on, on what this is meant to teach you.
